explanation.title: Comparing Values and Combining Conditions in Java
explanation.content:
# Comparison Operators

A condition inside an `if` statement must evaluate to a `boolean` — either `true` or `false`. Comparison operators produce this boolean result by comparing two values:

| Operator | Meaning | Example | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `==` | Equal to | `5 == 5` | `true` |
| `!=` | Not equal to | `5 != 3` | `true` |
| `>` | Greater than | `7 > 3` | `true` |
| `<` | Less than | `3 < 7` | `true` |
| `>=` | Greater or equal | `5 >= 5` | `true` |
| `<=` | Less or equal | `4 <= 3` | `false` |

**Critical rule:** Use `==` only for primitives. For `String` comparison, always use `.equals()`:

```java
"hello" == "hello"         // unreliable — compares references
"hello".equals("hello")    // always correct — compares content
```

## Logical Operators

Combine multiple boolean conditions:

| Operator | Meaning | Short-circuits? |
|---|---|---|
| `&&` | AND — both must be true | Yes — skips right if left is false |
| `||` | OR — at least one must be true | Yes — skips right if left is true |
| `!` | NOT — flips true/false | N/A |

```java
int age    = 25;
boolean hasId = true;

if (age >= 18 && hasId) {
    System.out.println("Access granted.");
}

if (!hasId || age < 18) {
    System.out.println("Access denied.");
}
```

## Short-Circuit Evaluation

With `&&`, if the left operand is `false`, Java never evaluates the right operand. With `||`, if the left is `true`, the right is skipped. This is called **short-circuit evaluation** and prevents errors:

```java
// Safe: if s is null, s.length() is never called
if (s != null && s.length() > 0) {
    System.out.println("Non-empty string.");
}
```

## Operator Precedence

In an expression with multiple operators:
1. `!` (NOT) — highest
2. `>`, `<`, `>=`, `<=` — comparisons
3. `==`, `!=` — equality checks
4. `&&` (AND)
5. `||` (OR) — lowest

Use parentheses to make complex conditions clear:
```java
if ((a > b && b > c) || isSpecialCase) { ... }
```

In the code example below, you will see logical operators used to build a complete bank loan eligibility checker.

codeExample.title: Loan Eligibility Checker
codeExample.explanation:
- `age >= 21 && age <= 60` — both comparisons must be true; short-circuits on first false
- `!hasLoan` — `!` flips `false` to `true`; eligible only when no existing loan exists
- `creditRating.equals("Good") || creditRating.equals("Excellent")` — `.equals()` compares String content; `||` needs only one true
- `ageOk && incomeOk && loanOk && creditOk` — all four must be true for loan approval
- `name != null && name.length() > 0` — short-circuit safety; if `name` is `null`, `.length()` is never called, preventing a NullPointerException

exercise.title: Build a Platform Access Validator
exercise.instructions:
Write a Java program that validates access to an online platform. Declare: int age = 16, boolean hasParentConsent = true, String membershipType = "free". Using logical operators, determine: isAgeValid (age >= 13), canAccess (isAgeValid && (age >= 18 || hasParentConsent)), isPremium (membershipType.equals("premium")). Print each boolean result and a final access message.

Expected: isAgeValid=true, canAccess=true, isPremium=false, access granted.
hints: 💡 Add an if/else block at the end: if (canAccess) { System.out.println("Access granted."); } else { ... }
💡 canAccess is already computed — just use it as the condition in your if statement.
💡 All the boolean logic is already done; you only need to print a message based on the canAccess result.

questions.question: What is short-circuit evaluation in Java's && operator?
options: Java evaluates both operands before checking the result
If the left operand is false, Java skips evaluating the right operand
The && operator only works with boolean primitives
Java evaluates the right operand first to improve performance
questions.explanation: With &&, if the left operand evaluates to false, the entire expression is already false regardless of the right operand. Java skips evaluating the right side entirely. This is useful for safety checks like obj != null && obj.method() — if obj is null, the method call is never made and no NullPointerException occurs.

questions.question: Why should you not use == to compare two String objects in Java?
options: == does not compile for reference types
== compares memory references, not character content, and may return false for equal strings
== only works when both strings are literals
questions.explanation: For objects like String, == compares whether both variables point to the exact same object in memory. Two String objects containing identical characters can exist at different memory locations, causing == to return false even though their content is the same. .equals() always compares the actual character sequences, reliably returning true for equal content.
